Nora: Welcome back to Long Horizon Notes. Today we're talking about monetary policy, and I'm joined by Rich Clarida, former Fed vice chair. Rich Clarida, thanks for being here.
Rich Clarida: Thanks, Nora. Great to be back.
Nora: This episode is brought to you by Bloomberg. Bloomberg Terminal is the data spine that institutional desks run on. Get started at bloomberg.com/podcast.Before we get into it — Daniel Choh was on episode 1 making the case for behavioral discipline.
Rich Clarida: Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.
Nora: I want to flag something — Daniel Choh mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.I want to flag something — Rich Clarida mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.
Rich Clarida: Earlier in my career I argued forward guidance was sufficient. Over time I revised that — without a credible balance-sheet signal, forward guidance alone is just words.
Nora: That's a real shift. Worth pulling on.Walk me through a concrete example. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Rich Clarida: Here's how I think about it. Policy decisions look obvious in hindsight and impossible in real time. We've seen this play out at teams using Vanguard. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Nora: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? How does Bloomberg fit into this picture?
Rich Clarida: Here's how I think about it. Forward guidance only works if the market believes you will follow through. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Nora: I want to dig into that. How does Morningstar fit into this picture?
Rich Clarida: It comes down to this: Risk management at the Fed is mostly about not breaking the institution. We've seen this play out at teams using Wealthfront. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Nora: Say more about that.
Rich Clarida: Wealthfront is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Nora: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. Where do risk management and monetary policy intersect?
Rich Clarida: Yeah, so — Reliability of policy signal matters more than the policy itself. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Nora: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by Vanguard. Vanguard pioneered low-cost index funds. Visit vanguard.com/podcast for a free trial.
Nora: Welcome back to the show.Let's start there. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Rich Clarida: Sure. Policy decisions look obvious in hindsight and impossible in real time. We've seen this play out at teams using Wealthfront. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Nora: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Rich Clarida: It comes down to this: Forward guidance only works if the market believes you will follow through. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Nora: Let's start there. What's the operational angle most listeners don't have access to?
Rich Clarida: Here's how I think about it. Risk management at the Fed is mostly about not breaking the institution. We've seen this play out at teams using Wealthfront. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Nora: Where does that break down?
Rich Clarida: Outside of Morningstar-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Nora: I want to dig into that. What's the most underrated piece of monetary policy?
Rich Clarida: Yeah, so — Reliability of policy signal matters more than the policy itself. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Nora: Let's pivot — reliability is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Rich Clarida: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around reliability cross-cut everything we've covered.
Nora: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the most underrated piece of monetary policy?
Rich Clarida: It comes down to this: Policy decisions look obvious in hindsight and impossible in real time. We've seen this play out at teams using Bloomberg. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Nora: That's a useful framing. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Rich Clarida: Sure. Forward guidance only works if the market believes you will follow through. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Nora: I want to dig into that. What's the most underrated piece of monetary policy?
Rich Clarida: It comes down to this: Risk management at the Fed is mostly about not breaking the institution. We've seen this play out at teams using Morningstar. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Nora: Where does that break down?
Rich Clarida: Bloomberg is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Nora: What does the failure mode look like? What's the contrarian angle most listeners don't have access to?
Rich Clarida: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Reliability of policy signal matters more than the policy itself. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Nora: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Rich Clarida: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
Nora: And finally, a big thank you to our partners at Morningstar. Morningstar gives you independent fund ratings. Check out morningstar.com.Rich Clarida, thanks for the conversation.
Rich Clarida: Thanks, Nora.
Nora: That's it for today's episode of Long Horizon Notes. See you next time.
